q: Summarize the book of Job: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Author unknown; the events are patriarchal in flavor (wealth measured in livestock, Job acting as family priest), so many place the setting in the patriarchal era, with composition possibly under Solomon's wisdom flourishing (c. 10th century B.C.) or earlier.

**Genre.** Wisdom; mostly poetic dialogue framed by prose narrative.

**Theme.** Why the righteous suffer, and whether God may be trusted and worshiped for His own sake rather than for blessings. The book answers not with tidy explanations but with God Himself, who is sovereign, wise, and beyond human cross-examination.

**Outline.**
- 1–2 — Prologue: Job's testing in the heavenly court
- 3 — Job's lament
- 4–27 — Three cycles of debate with the friends
- 28–31 — Wisdom hymn and Job's final defense
- 32–37 — Elihu's speeches
- 38–42 — The LORD answers; Job's repentance and restoration

**Christ & significance.** Job longs for a mediator and living Redeemer (9:33; 19:25), a cry answered fully in Christ, the truly innocent sufferer whose vindication secures ours.
summary: Author unknown, patriarchal setting (possibly composed in the Solomonic era). A righteous man's suffering exposes the limits of human wisdom and ends in God's self-revelation and Job's restoration.

q: Summarize the Psalms: authorship, date, theme, and structure.
a:
**Author & date.** Multiple authors over roughly a millennium: David (the largest share), Asaph, the sons of Korah, Solomon, Moses (Psalm 90), and others, with anonymous psalms as well; composed from the time of Moses through the post-exilic period and gathered into its final shape by the Second Temple era.

**Genre.** Poetry; the inspired hymnbook and prayer book of Israel.

**Theme.** The full range of covenant life lived before God — praise, lament, thanksgiving, confession, and trust — teaching God's people to worship Him as sovereign King and faithful Redeemer in every circumstance.

**Outline.**
- 1–41 — Book I
- 42–72 — Book II
- 73–89 — Book III
- 90–106 — Book IV
- 107–150 — Book V

**Christ & significance.** The Psalter is richly messianic; the New Testament reads it as pointing to Christ as the suffering and reigning King (Psalm 2; 22; 110), and the church prays and sings it as the prayers Christ Himself takes up on our behalf.
summary: Many authors including David, written from Moses' day to after the exile and arranged in five books. Israel's inspired hymnbook of praise and lament, read by the church as deeply messianic.

q: Summarize the book of Proverbs: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Primarily Solomon (10th century B.C.), with named additions from the wise, from Agur, and from King Lemuel; the Solomonic core was later supplemented and copied by Hezekiah's men (25:1), giving the book its final form.

**Genre.** Wisdom; pithy poetic sayings.

**Theme.** The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom — practical, God-centered instruction in skillful, righteous living that applies covenant truth to everyday choices of speech, work, money, sex, and relationships.

**Outline.**
- 1–9 — Extended discourses commending wisdom over folly
- 10–22:16 — Proverbs of Solomon
- 22:17–24:34 — Sayings of the wise
- 25–29 — Further Solomonic proverbs copied by Hezekiah's men
- 30 — Words of Agur
- 31 — Words of Lemuel and the excellent wife

**Christ & significance.** Wisdom personified (chapter 8) anticipates Christ, who is made to us wisdom from God (1 Corinthians 1:30); true skill in living is finally found in Him.
summary: Primarily Solomon, c. 10th century B.C., with later collections. God-centered wisdom for daily life rooted in the fear of the LORD, anticipating Christ who is our wisdom.

q: Summarize the book of Ecclesiastes: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Traditionally Solomon, the "Preacher" (Qoheleth), son of David and king in Jerusalem; composed in the 10th century B.C., reflecting the perspective of one with unmatched wealth, wisdom, and opportunity to test life's pursuits.

**Genre.** Wisdom; reflective discourse.

**Theme.** Apart from God, all earthly pursuits are "vanity" — fleeting and unable to satisfy. Life "under the sun" cannot be mastered or fully understood, so the wise response is to fear God, keep His commandments, and gratefully enjoy His gifts.

**Outline.**
- 1:1–11 — Theme: the vanity of life under the sun
- 1:12–2:26 — The Preacher's search for meaning
- 3:1–6:12 — Time, injustice, and the limits of wealth
- 7:1–11:6 — Wisdom for living in a fallen world
- 11:7–12:8 — Remember your Creator in youth
- 12:9–14 — Conclusion: fear God and keep His commandments

**Christ & significance.** By exposing the emptiness of life without God, Ecclesiastes drives readers to find lasting meaning beyond the sun in Christ, in whom creation's frustration is finally redeemed.
summary: Traditionally Solomon, c. 10th century B.C. Everything "under the sun" is fleeting; the answer is to fear God, enjoy His gifts, and look beyond this life to Christ.

q: Summarize the Song of Songs: author, date, theme, and structure.
a:
**Author & date.** Primarily Solomon, named in the title (1:1); composed in the 10th century B.C. as "the song of songs," the finest of his many songs.

**Genre.** Poetry; lyric love poetry.

**Theme.** A celebration of the goodness, beauty, and exclusive faithfulness of love between husband and wife within marriage as God designed it, honoring romantic and physical love as a good gift to be guarded and rightly desired.

**Outline.**
- 1:1 — Title
- 1:2–2:7 — Mutual longing and delight of the lovers
- 2:8–3:5 — Courtship and seeking the beloved
- 3:6–5:1 — The wedding and its consummation
- 5:2–8:4 — Separation, renewed desire, and reunion
- 8:5–14 — The strength and seal of covenant love

**Christ & significance.** The church has long read the Song as portraying covenant love, and beyond marriage as a window onto the faithful, ardent love between Christ and His bride, the church (Ephesians 5:25–32).
summary: Primarily Solomon, c. 10th century B.C. A poetic celebration of marital love, read by the church as a picture of covenant love and of Christ's love for His bride.
